clinical_history: ECG from an elderly man with a rapid heartbeat. What is the diagnosis?
diagnosis: The ECG shows a narrow complex tachycardia with a very regular ventricular response at 140/min. The atrial depolarization rate is twice that--280/min--with classical sawtooth flutter waves apparent in lead II. The polarity of the atrial waves (negative inferiorly and positive in V1) is consistent with typical (isthmus-dependent) flutter originating in the right atrium, associated with a large (macro-) counterclockwise, reentrant loop. The phase relationship (flutter-R interval) is constant, consistent with pure  2:1 AV conduction. The ECG also shows a QRS axis within the physiologic range (about 0 degrees) and a normal QRS interval (about 90 ms), with non-specific ST-T changes. R wave progression is slow, a non-diagnostic finding. The QTc is not prolonged.  The patient had a history of atherosclerotic vascular disease and systemic hypertension.
